title: Finance & Banking Vocabulary
description: Essential vocabulary for finance, banking, investing, and economic concepts used in everyday and professional contexts.

cards.meaning: a record of money held at a bank
cards.example: I opened a new bank account last week.

cards.meaning: the amount of money in a bank account
cards.example: Check your account balance before making a payment.

cards.meaning: money placed into a bank account
cards.example: I made a deposit of five hundred dollars.

cards.meaning: to take money out of a bank account
cards.example: She withdrew cash from the ATM.

cards.meaning: to move money from one account to another
cards.example: I need to transfer money to my savings account.

cards.meaning: the system of money used in a country
cards.example: The local currency is the Japanese yen.

cards.meaning: the value of one currency in terms of another
cards.example: The exchange rate today is 1.25 dollars per euro.

cards.meaning: a general rise in prices over time
cards.example: Inflation has made everyday goods more expensive.

cards.meaning: the percentage charged on a loan or paid on savings
cards.example: The bank increased its interest rate this quarter.

cards.meaning: a loan secured against a property
cards.example: They took out a 30-year mortgage to buy the house.

cards.meaning: money borrowed that must be paid back
cards.example: She applied for a personal loan to pay for tuition.

cards.meaning: the ability to borrow money or a positive bank entry
cards.example: He has a good credit history, so he got a low rate.

cards.meaning: money that is owed to someone else
cards.example: They worked hard to pay off their debt.

cards.meaning: money put into something to earn more money
cards.example: Property is considered a safe long-term investment.

cards.meaning: a collection of investments owned by a person
cards.example: Her portfolio includes stocks, bonds, and real estate.

cards.meaning: a portion of a company's profit paid to shareholders
cards.example: Shareholders receive a dividend twice a year.

cards.meaning: a share of ownership in a company
cards.example: He bought stock in a technology company.

cards.meaning: a debt investment where you lend money to an entity
cards.example: Government bonds are considered safe investments.

cards.meaning: the value of shares or ownership in an asset
cards.example: She has built up significant equity in her home.

cards.meaning: something of value owned by a person or company
cards.example: Property and cash are important assets.

cards.meaning: a financial debt or obligation
cards.example: The company's liabilities exceeded its assets.

cards.meaning: a plan for spending and managing money
cards.example: We need to set a budget for the project.

cards.meaning: money spent on something
cards.example: Travel is the biggest expense in our budget.

cards.meaning: the income generated by a business
cards.example: The company's annual revenue grew by fifteen percent.

cards.meaning: money made after all expenses are deducted
cards.example: The business made a large profit last quarter.

cards.meaning: when costs exceed income
cards.example: The company reported a loss for the third year in a row.

cards.meaning: money paid to the government based on income or goods
cards.example: Income tax is deducted from your salary automatically.

cards.meaning: an amount subtracted from income before tax is calculated
cards.example: Charitable donations can be used as tax deductions.

cards.meaning: money returned to a customer or taxpayer
cards.example: She received a tax refund after filing her return.

cards.meaning: a document requesting payment for goods or services
cards.example: The supplier sent an invoice for the order.

cards.meaning: a document proving a payment was made
cards.example: Keep your receipt in case you want to return the item.

cards.meaning: an instance of buying or selling
cards.example: All transactions are recorded on your statement.

cards.meaning: a charge for a service
cards.example: There is a fee for using the ATM abroad.

cards.meaning: a financial punishment for breaking a rule
cards.example: There is a penalty for late payment of bills.

cards.meaning: protection against financial loss
cards.example: Car insurance is required by law.

cards.meaning: the regular payment made for insurance
cards.example: Your monthly insurance premium is $120.

cards.meaning: the amount you pay before insurance covers the rest
cards.example: The policy has a $500 deductible.

cards.meaning: a person who receives money from an insurance or will
cards.example: She named her daughter as the beneficiary.

cards.meaning: the period after you stop working permanently
cards.example: He is saving money for a comfortable retirement.

cards.meaning: regular payments made to someone who has retired
cards.example: She receives a state pension every month.

cards.meaning: a fixed sum paid yearly, often after retirement
cards.example: He invested in an annuity to secure income after retiring.

cards.meaning: interest calculated on initial amount plus accumulated interest
cards.example: Compound interest helps your savings grow faster over time.

cards.meaning: Annual Percentage Rate; the yearly cost of borrowing
cards.example: Compare the APR before choosing a credit card.

cards.meaning: spending more money than is in your bank account
cards.example: He went into overdraft after an unexpected expense.

cards.meaning: an asset used as security for a loan
cards.example: The house was used as collateral for the loan.

cards.meaning: the legal state of being unable to repay debts
cards.example: The company filed for bankruptcy after years of losses.

cards.meaning: converting assets into cash, often to pay debts
cards.example: The firm went into liquidation after failing to find buyers.

cards.meaning: an official inspection of financial accounts
cards.example: The company underwent an annual financial audit.

cards.meaning: a book or record of financial transactions
cards.example: All income and expenses are recorded in the ledger.

cards.meaning: the movement of money into and out of a business
cards.example: Good cash flow is essential for a healthy business.

cards.meaning: total assets minus total liabilities
cards.example: His net worth increased significantly after the property sale.

cards.meaning: total earnings before any deductions
cards.example: Her gross income is $60,000 per year.

cards.meaning: earnings after taxes and deductions
cards.example: His net income is what actually hits his bank account.

cards.meaning: the list of employees and amounts they are paid
cards.example: HR manages the monthly payroll for all staff.

cards.meaning: extra money paid in addition to salary
cards.example: She received a performance bonus at the end of the year.

cards.meaning: a fee paid based on sales or performance
cards.example: Sales staff earn a commission on every deal they close.

cards.meaning: the lowest legal hourly rate an employer can pay
cards.example: The government raised the minimum wage this year.

cards.meaning: the amount of money needed to cover basic expenses
cards.example: The cost of living in the city is very high.

cards.meaning: a measure of inflation based on typical consumer goods
cards.example: The consumer price index rose by 3% last year.

cards.meaning: Gross Domestic Product; total value of a country's output
cards.example: The country's GDP grew by 2.5% this year.

cards.meaning: a period of economic decline lasting several months
cards.example: Many people lost their jobs during the recession.

cards.meaning: government spending to boost a struggling economy
cards.example: The government announced an economic stimulus package.

cards.meaning: money given by the government to support an industry
cards.example: Farmers receive subsidies to keep food prices stable.

cards.meaning: a tax placed on imported goods
cards.example: The tariff on steel increased the cost of construction.

cards.meaning: goods brought into a country from abroad
cards.example: The country imports most of its electronics from Asia.

cards.meaning: goods sold and sent to another country
cards.example: Coffee is one of Brazil's main exports.

cards.meaning: when imports exceed exports in value
cards.example: The country is running a large trade deficit.

cards.meaning: government decisions on taxes and spending
cards.example: Fiscal policy was adjusted to combat rising unemployment.

cards.meaning: central bank actions to control money supply and rates
cards.example: Monetary policy aims to keep inflation under control.

cards.meaning: a national institution that manages currency and monetary policy
cards.example: The central bank raised interest rates to fight inflation.

cards.meaning: the central banking system of the United States
cards.example: The Federal Reserve cut rates to stimulate the economy.

cards.meaning: a digital currency not controlled by any government
cards.example: Bitcoin is the most well-known cryptocurrency.

cards.meaning: a digital record of transactions shared across a network
cards.example: Blockchain technology underpins most cryptocurrencies.

cards.meaning: financial services using technology and software
cards.example: Many fintech startups are disrupting traditional banking.

cards.meaning: an app that stores payment information for digital transactions
cards.example: She pays for everything using her digital wallet.

cards.meaning: a payment made by tapping a card or device
cards.example: Contactless payment is fast and convenient.

cards.meaning: sending money electronically from one bank to another
cards.example: She sent the payment via wire transfer.

cards.meaning: gradually paying off a loan through regular payments
cards.example: The mortgage amortization schedule shows monthly payments.

cards.meaning: the reduction in value of an asset over time
cards.example: Car depreciation is fastest in the first year.

cards.meaning: an increase in the value of an asset over time
cards.example: Property appreciation made them wealthy over the years.

cards.meaning: profit made from selling an asset at a higher price
cards.example: She paid tax on the capital gains from selling her shares.

cards.meaning: an investment fund that uses strategies to reduce risk
cards.example: Hedge funds are typically for wealthy investors.

cards.meaning: a pool of money invested by many people together
cards.example: Mutual funds spread risk across many investments.

cards.meaning: a fund that tracks a stock market index
cards.example: Index funds offer low fees and broad market exposure.

cards.meaning: Initial Public Offering; a company's first stock sale to the public
cards.example: The tech firm raised billions in its IPO.

cards.meaning: money kept aside and not spent
cards.example: She put part of each paycheck into her savings account.
